docs.title: What is Zephyr?
docs.text:
Zephyr is a unique programming language designed around variables as the primary concept. Unlike traditional languages where functions and classes are the focus, Zephyr puts variables at the center of everything. This makes it perfect for learning how programming languages actually work under the hood.

All variables in Zephyr are lowercase, and the syntax is designed to be minimal yet powerful. You can use Zephyr to: - Store and manipulate data - Perform calculations - Build logic with conditions and loops - Create reusable code with functions - Interact with files and hardware

docs.title: For Learners
docs.text: If you're interested in understanding how programming languages work internally - how they parse code, compile it, and execute it - Zephyr gives you hands-on experience with these concepts. By learning Zephyr, you'll develop a deeper understanding of programming fundamentals that applies to any language.

docs.title: For Educators
docs.text: If you teach programming, Zephyr provides a fresh perspective on teaching core programming concepts. The variable-centric approach makes it easier to explain what happens "behind the scenes" when code runs.

docs.title: For Curious Developers
docs.text: If you've always wondered how compilation and execution really work, Zephyr demystifies these processes with practical, working examples.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Variable-Centric Design: Variables are not just containers for data - they're the foundation of the entire language structure
  • Educational Focus: Every feature is designed to teach you something about how programming languages work
  • Simple Syntax: Minimal, clear syntax means less time learning syntax rules and more time understanding concepts
  • Rich Documentation: This guide includes plenty of examples to help you learn by doing

docs.title: Who Should Use This?
docs.text:
• Intermediate developers wanting to expand their understanding of programming language design
  • Experienced programmers exploring innovative teaching approaches
  • Curious learners interested in diving deeper into how languages work
  • Students and educators looking for hands-on language learning experiences

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Ready to dive in? Head over to the Getting Started Guide to write your first Zephyr program..

docs.title: Other
docs.text:
• Build .zim documentation: 
    mkdocs serve

docker run \
--network host \
  -v $(pwd)/docs:/output \
  ghcr.io/openzim/zimit \
    zimit \
      --seeds http://localhost:8000/Zephyr \
      --name ZephyrDocs.zim \
      --title "Zephyr Documentation" \
      --creator "DeyanM1" \
      --publisher "DeyanM1" \
      --lang "en" \
      --description "Full documentation of the Zephyr programming Language by DeyanM1"

docs.title: Error Codes
docs.text: Code Name Description 101 UnknownBase Unknown Base, use '{ZBase.use}' or '{ZBase.define}' 102 UndefinedVariable Undefined Variable 103 UnknownFunction Unknown Function. 104 InvalidStructure Wrong command structure. Missing ':' or ';'? 105 ValueError Variable Type doesn't match given Value 106 ValueError Invalid Boolean type. Allowed: ~0 / ~1 107 WriteProtection Value cannot be changed. Variable is constant! 108 TypeError Current Variable type doesnt support new variable type! 109 ListIndexError List doesn't support position 0! 110 WriteError Only INT, PT, FLOAT are in- and decrementable! 111 ConditionError Error in Condition/Equation. 112 ParamUnsupportedTypeError Given variable isnt correct type! 113 ParamUndefinedVariable Given variable isnt defined! 114 ParamError Error in arguments! 115 JumpOutOfBounds Error at jump function! Index out of range! 116 MissingFile file cannot be found! 117 UnsupportedFile Target file isnt a correct type! 118 SyntaxError Error at first line of .zph file 119 ListOutOfBounds Error at Listindex. Index out of bounds 120 ListTypeError Some Value in List doesnt match new Type 121 ClassMissingFunctionReg Class error! Variable doesnt have Function Registry! 122 InvalidIndexError PT insertion Error! Index can't be smaller than 1 123 IndexOutoFBounds PT insertion Error! Index out of bounds! 124 ModuleNotFound Module Not Found inside global / local dir 125 UnknownListCollectionType Unknown List collection type! Use: POS / NEG 126 UncompletedIndexScobe Uncompleted Index Scobe: Missing > in variable index. 127 MathObjectNotFound MO name not yet provided. use: ? w. 128 KeyboardInterrupt Program exited by KeyboardInterrupt 129 UnknownPlaceholder Placeholder not found

docs.title: The Basic Structure of Every Command
docs.text:
In Zephyr, every statement follows the same pattern:

<VariableName> <base> <function>:<Argument1>|<Argument2>|...;
<---------------------------command------------------------->

Here's what each part means:

  • VariableName: The name of the variable, user-defined.
  • base: The base of the variable, used to define its operation:
  • ?: For variable operations (e.g., modifying or retrieving values).
  • #: For declaring variables.
  • function: The operation to execute on the variable (e.g., printing, defining a function).
  • arguments: Additional information passed to the function Seperated by |.
  • command: The entire statement.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
counter # INT:10;
Part Meaning counter This is the name of our variable # We're creating/declaring this variable INT The type is Integer (whole numbers) :10 The initial value is 10 ; End of statement

docs.title: File Extensions
docs.text: When you save Zephyr programs, use these file types:

docs.title: Running a Script File
docs.text:
If you have a file called program.zph:

python src/zcli.py program.zph

docs.title: Interactive Shell Mode
docs.text:
To try commands interactively without a file:

python src/zcli.py

Type your commands at the prompt. Each command runs immediately. Type exit to quit.

Important: If you press Ctrl+C instead of exit, temporary files won't be cleaned up, this is useful for keeping .zsrc files.

docs.title: Comments: Documenting Your Code
docs.text:
Comments help explain what your code does. In Zephyr, use § (section symbol) to mark comments:

counter # INT:10;  § This is a comment on the same line
§ This entire line is a comment and will be ignored

docs.title: Parameters Are Absolute Order
docs.text: When a function expects parameters, you must provide them in the exact order specified.

docs.title: Optional Parameters
docs.text:
Some functions have optional parameters. These are shown with a - in documentation:

function:<*- OptionalParam>

If you don't provide optional parameters, Zephyr uses default values.

docs.title: Variables in Arguments
docs.text:
You can use variables as arguments by enclosing them in single quotes:

num1 # INT:5;
num2 # INT:10;
sum # INT:0;
sum ? w:('num1' + 'num2');  § Using variables num1 and num2

The 'num1' and 'num2' are replaced with their actual values.

docs.title: Wildcards in Documentation
docs.text:
In the documentation, you'll see symbols representing different things:

Symbol Meaning * A value (can be a value compatible with the variable type or variable name in quotes) - An optional value ' ' Enclose variable names in single quotes when using them as values

docs.title: Convertible information
docs.text:
Every type has a set of other types it can be converted into. In the documentation Allowed Conversions are declared using the convertibleInto property, followed by the list of target types:

ConvertValue is the value that is going to be passed onto the new Variable type

  • convertibleInto -> PT, INT
  • convertValue -> Value of the Var

Note: Even if the variable is compatible with the new type, the conversion can fail because the value of the type is not compatible with the new type.

docs.title: Declaring Variables
docs.text:
To create a new variable, use the # base:

<VariableName> # <Type>:<Value>|<Param1>|<Param2>|...;

Example:

age # INT:25;
name # PT:"Alice";
active # BOOL:~1;

docs.title: Changing a Variable's Type
docs.text:
You can convert a variable from one type to another using ? CT: (Change Type):

count # INT:5;
count ? CT:PT;  § Convert from INT to PT (now it's text "5")

Not all type conversions are possible. Check the documentation for each type to see which conversions are allowed.

Note: You can also just redeclare a variable with a new type using #:

value # INT:10;
value # PT:"Hello";  § Redeclare as a different type

docs.title: Working with Variables
docs.text:
Once you create a variable, you use ? to perform actions on it:

message # PT:"Hello";
message ? push:;              § Display the message
message ? w:"Goodbye";        § Change the value

docs.title: Zephyr Library Manager (ZLM)
docs.text: The Zephyr Library Manager (zlm.py) is a tool for installing and removing libraries. Libraries extend Zephyr's functionality with additional features.

docs.title: Understanding Libraries
docs.text:
• Libraries are new Variable Types.
  • Libraries must be imported into your program before you can use them.

docs.title: Basic Installation
docs.text:
Install libraries to a specific directory:

python zlm.py install --path "src/lib/" GPIO.py moreMath.py

This command: - Installs GPIO.py and moreMath.py - Places them in the src/lib/ directory - Makes them available for local projects - creates a libs.txt file at the insall location, adds newly installed libs to that file

docs.title: Default Installation
docs.text:
If you don't specify a path, libraries go to a lib directory in your current working directory:

python zlm.py install <LibraryName>
python zlm.py install GPIO.py

Creates: ./lib/GPIO.py

docs.title: Global Installation
docs.text:
Install libraries system-wide (available to all Zephyr projects):

python zlm.py install --global <LibraryName>
python zlm.py install --global GPIO.py

Note: Global installation is disabled by default for security.

docs.title: Uninstalling Libraries
docs.text:
Remove locally installed libraries:

python zlm.py remove <LibraryName>
python zlm.py remove GPIO.py
  • removes the file from the libs.txt Important: To remove a library from the global installation location include the --global flag.

docs.title: Update Libraries
docs.text:
update installed library

python zlm.py update <LibraryName>
  • if no names reinstalls all libs written inside libs.txt
  • can only update if lib is inside of libs.txt
  • --global flag can be used

docs.title: Using Libraries
docs.text:
Once installed, import a library using the built-in LIB command:

__ ? LIB:./lib/GPIO.py;

There are three options on how to import a lib:

docs.title: 1. Absolute Path:
docs.text: __ ? LIB:/home/<User>/libs/GPIO.py;

docs.title: 2. Relative Path:
docs.text:
• Looks in the current working dir inside myLibs 
    __ ? LIB:./myLibs/GPIO.py;

docs.title: 3. Bare Name:
docs.text:
• Looks inside of the global install dir, if none found it looks inside of local ./lib dir in the cwd 
    __ ? LIB:GPIO.py;

After importing, you can use the library's features (see library-specific documentation).

docs.title: Using Libraries
docs.text:
Import a library into your Zephyr script using the built-in LIB command:

__ ? LIB:GPIO.py;

Zephyr resolves the library path in one of the three ways depending on waht you provide:

docs.title: 1. Absolute Path
docs.text:
Provide a full path starting from the filesystem root. Zephyr uses it directly with no additional lookup.

__ ? LIB:/home/user/libs/GPIO.py;

docs.title: 2. Relative Path
docs.text:
Provide a apth containing a separator (e.g. ./, ../, or a subfolder). Zephyr resolves it relative to the directory of the current .zphfile, not the working directory of the process.

__ ? LIB:./myLibs/GPIO.py;
__ ? LIB:../shared/GPIO.py;

docs.title: 3. Bare Name
docs.text:
Provide just a filename with no path separators. Zephyr searches in this order:

  1. Global library directory - the path set by you Zephyr installation (e.g ~/.config/Zephyr/libs/)
  2. Local lib/ directory - a lib/fodler next to the current .zph file

The first match wins. If neither location contains the file, en error is raised.

__ ? LIB:GPIO.py;

Tip: The bare name is the recommended way to use officially installed libraries. Use relative paths for project-local modules.

docs.title: Organizing Libraries
docs.text:
Keep libraries organized in your project:

my_project/
  ├── src/
  │   ├── main.zph
  │   └── lib/
  │       ├── GPIO.py
  │       ├── python.py
  └─      └── system.py

docs.title: Multiple Libraries at Once
docs.text: python zlm.py install --path "src/lib/" GPIO.py python.py system.py

docs.title: Advanced: Creating Custom Libraries
docs.text: You can create your own libraries by writing Python code. See each library's documentation for structure and examples.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Task Command Install local python zlm.py install --path "src/lib/" LIB.py Install global python zlm.py install --global LIB.py Uninstall python zlm.py uninstall LIB.py Use in program __ ? LIB:./lib/LIB.py;

docs.title: GPIO
docs.text: The GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) library allows Zephyr programs to interface with hardware pins. It supports pin configuration, reading input signals, writing output signals, and cleanup operations. The variable's value represents the status of the last read pin.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, FLOAT, INT, BOOL
  • convertValue -> State of last read Pin

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Before using GPIO, import the library:

__ ? LIB:"GPIO.py";

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a GPIO interface with the specified pinout type.

gpio # GPIO:<*PinoutType>; 
gpio # GPIO:BCM;
  • PinoutType — Pin numbering scheme: BCM or Board

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
change the Pinout Type

gpio ? w:<*PinoutType>;
gpio ? w:Board;
  • PinoutType — Pin numbering scheme: BCM or Board

docs.title: Setup Pin (SETUP)
docs.text:
Configures a pin for input or output.

gpio ? SETUP:<*PinNum>|<*PinType>;
gpio ? SETUP:17|OUT;

PinType options: - IN — Input mode - OUT — Output mode

docs.title: Set Pin Value (SET)
docs.text:
Writes a digital value to an output pin.

gpio ? SET:<*PinNum>|<*PinValue>;
gpio ? SET:17|1;

PinValue options: - 1 — HIGH (logic 1) - 0 — LOW (logic 0)

docs.title: Read Pin Value (READ)
docs.text:
Reads the digital value from an input pin. The result is stored in the GPIO variable's value field.

gpio ? READ:<*PinNum>;
gpio ? READ:17;

The pin must be configured as IN before reading.

docs.title: Clean All Pins (CLEAN)
docs.text:
Resets and cleans up all pins, returning them to default state.

gpio ? CLEAN:;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Choose the correct pinout type (BCM or Board) based on your hardware documentation.
  • Input pins must be configured with SETUP before calling READ.
  • Output pins must be configured with SETUP before calling SET.
  • Always call CLEAN: at the end of your program to properly release hardware resources.
  • Pin numbers vary depending on the chosen pinout scheme.

docs.title: Python
docs.text: The Python library allows Zephyr programs to execute Python code directly from within Zephyr scripts. This enables integration with Python libraries and advanced system operations.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Before using Python, import the library:

__ ? LIB:"python.py";

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a Python interface. No arguments are needed.

python # PYTHON:;

docs.title: Run (run)
docs.text:
Executes a Python command or statement.

python ? run:<* pythonCommand>;
python ? run:(print("Hello World"));

docs.title: Python Command Format
docs.text:
Python commands are written in Zephyr as follows:

  • Enclose Python code in parentheses
  • Use double quotes instead of single quotes for strings
  • To use a Zephyr variable value, reference it with quotes

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Python commands run with full access to Python standard library.
  • Use double quotes for Python strings, not single quotes.
  • Be careful when executing untrusted Python code, as it runs with the same permissions as the Zephyr interpreter.

docs.title: System
docs.text: The System library provides control over basic system behavior, including program termination and working directory management.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Before using System, import the library:

__ ? LIB:system.py;

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a System interface. No arguments are needed.

system # SYSTEM:;

docs.title: Quit
docs.text:
Terminates the program with an optional error code. Default error code is 0 (success).

system ? quit:<*- ErrorCode>;
system ? quit:0;

ErrorCode values: - 0 — Success (program completed normally) - Any non-zero integer — Indicates an error or failure condition

docs.title: Get Current Working Directory (getCWD)
docs.text:
Retrieves the current working directory and stores it in a text variable.

system ? getCWD:<* variableNameToSaveTo>;
system ? getCWD:pathVariable;

docs.title: Ascii
docs.text: Ascii provides simple functions to turn ascii codes to chars and reverse.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> None
  • convertValue -> Cant convert

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Before using ascii, import the library:

__ ? LIB:ascii.py;

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a Ascii interface. No arguments are needed.

ascii # ASCII:;

docs.title: ToAscii
docs.text:
Converts a character into a ascii code

ascii ? ToAscii:<* CharToConvert>|<* TargetVarName>;
  • CharToConvert: Char to convert (Var Type: PT)
  • TargetVarName: Name of the var where the Num is pasted into (Var Type: INT/FLOAT)

docs.title: ToChar
docs.text:
Converts an Ascii Code into a char

ascii ? ToChar:<* NumToConvert>|<* TargetVarName>;
  • NumToConvert: Num to convert (Var Type INT/FLOAT)
  • TargetVarName: Name of the var where the char is pasted into

docs.title: Math
docs.text: Math provides usueful mathematic functions.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> None

  • convertValue -> Cant convert

  • INFO: when using a function the caluclated var is written to the variable entered

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Before using Math, import the library:

__ ? LIB:MATH.py;

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a MATH interface. No arguments are needed.

math # MATH:;

docs.title: Factorial
docs.text:
Calculate the Factorial of the value at the given Var.

math ? fact:<* variableName>;
math ? fact:myVar;

docs.title: Absolute
docs.text:
Calculate the Absolute of the value at the given Var.

math ? abs:<* variableName>;
math ? abs:myVar;

docs.title: PI
docs.text:
Set the Value of PI at the given Var.

math ? setPi:<* variableName>;
math ? setPi:myVar;

docs.title: sqrt
docs.text:
Calculate the square Root of the value at the given Var.

math ? sqrt:<* variableName>;
math ? sqrt:myVar;

docs.title: Max
docs.text:
Get the biggest number of the positive Collection of a List

math ? max:<*- listVarName>|<*- TargetVarName>;
math ? max:myList|biggestNum;

docs.title: Blank Library
docs.text:
Every library needs an importHandler that correctly imports the required classes (e.g. ZCommand) from base.py. If you instead import directly with from base import <name>, it will not work when the library is loaded through the zlm installer. A direct import can still be used temporarily for type validation while debugging, but must be commented out (or removed) before shipping the library.

The library's main class must extend Base (also imported via the importHandler).

It must also define a load() function returning a dict[str, type], e.g.:

{"myFunc": MyModule}

The key is an optional alternative name for the type and must not contain spaces. If left as an empty string (""), the class name (MyModule) is used instead.

docs.title: __init__
docs.text:
The main class's __init__ must accept self, cmd, activeVars, and must call self.registerFunc({...}).

registerFunc takes a dict mapping function names to callables, e.g.:

{"funcName": callable}

If a key is left as an empty string, the name of the class is used as the function name instead.

docs.title: Blank template
docs.text:
from __future__ import annotations
#from base import ZCommand, ActiveVars, Base    # <- for debugging only. Use importHandler for the final project!

def importHandler(names: list[str]):
    import importlib.util
    from pathlib import Path
    import sys
    base_path = Path(__file__).resolve().parent / "base.py"
    moduleName = base_path.stem
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(moduleName, base_path)
    if spec is None or spec.loader is None:
        raise ImportError(f"Failed to create spec for {base_path}")
    base = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    sys.modules[moduleName] = base
    spec.loader.exec_module(base)
    for name in names:
        globals()[name] = getattr(base, name)

importHandler(["ZCommand", "ActiveVars", "base"])

class Blank(Base):
    def __init__(self, cmd: ZCommand, activeVars: ActiveVars) -> None:
        super().__init__(cmd, activeVars)
        self.registerFunc({})

def load() -> dict[str, type]:
    return {"": Blank}

docs.title: Simple Variables
docs.text: Simple variables are the most basic building blocks in Zephyr. They store single pieces of data that can be modified, displayed, or used in calculations.

docs.title: Variable Types
docs.text:
Zephyr supports four types of simple variables:

Type Purpose Example PT Printable Text - strings of characters Hello World INT Integer - whole numbers 42, -10 FLOAT Floating-point - decimal numbers 3.14, -2.5 BOOL Boolean - true or false values ~1 (true) or ~0 (false)

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT, BOOL, LIST
  • convertValue -> Value of variable

Note: Every simplevar can be converted between the 4 simplevar types Note: Only PT can be turned into LIST, Chars of the PT are turned into values of LIST (Split by char)

docs.title: Creating a Simple Variable
docs.text:
To create a simple variable, use the # base with the type, optional initial value and optional constant flag:

<VariableName> # <Type>:<*- Value>|<*- const>;

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
age # INT:25|~0;           § Create an integer
name # PT:"Alice";         § Create text
pi # FLOAT:3.14;           § Create a decimal number
is_active # BOOL:~1;       § Create a boolean (true)

docs.title: Changing Variable Types
docs.text:
You can convert a simple variable to a different type using ? CT: (Change Type):

value # INT:5;
value ? CT:PT;             § Convert from INT to PT

docs.title: Conversion Rules
docs.text:
When converting, here's what happens:

From To Result Any type PT Converts the values to text PT INT or FLOAT Only works if text is a valid number BOOL:~1 INT or FLOAT Becomes 1 or 1.0 BOOL:~0 INT or FLOAT Becomes 0 or 0.0 Any type BOOL Only works if the value is 1, 0, ~1, or ~0

docs.title: Constant
docs.text:
You can make a simple variable constant (unchangeable) by adding the ~1 parameter:

<name> # <Type>:<*- initialValue>|<*- const>

pi # PT:3.14159|~1;      § This variable cannot be changed

If you try to change a constant variable, you'll get an error:

pi # PT:3.14159|~1;
pi ? w:3.14;             § ERROR! Write Protection - can't change constant

Or using the ? C:

<name> ? C:<~ const>;

age # INT:25|~1;
age ? w:26;               § Throws a write protection error
age ? C:~0;               § write protection is now turned off
age ? w:26;               § Value is now changed.
  • const is a boolean value that sets if the var is const or not.

docs.title: Length of a variable
docs.text:
use LNGH to get the amount of characters or digits in a variable

this function is available on: INT, PT, FLOAT

usage:

<name> ? LGTH:<* targetVarName>;

a # PT:Length;
lengthOfA # INT:;

a ? LGTH:lengthOfA;

lengthOfA ? push:;    § outputs 6
  • targetVarName is the name of the variable the length is pasted into

docs.title: Creating Text Variables
docs.text:
<name> # PT:<*- initialValue>|<*- const>;

greeting # PT:Hello;
message # PT:Welcome to Zephyr;

docs.title: Displaying Text
docs.text:
Use ? push: to display text:

<name> ? push:<*- newLine>;

greeting # PT:Hello;
greeting ? push:;          § Output: Hello
  • newLine if set to False it doesnt print a newline after the print (~1 / ~0). Default is True
  • only Variables of the Type PT can be pushed

docs.title: Changing Text Value
docs.text:
Use ? w: to write/change the value:

<name> ? w:<newValue>;

message # PT:Hello;
message ? w:Goodbye;     § Now message contains "Goodbye"
message ? push:;         § Output: Goodbye

docs.title: Adding Text (Incrementing)
docs.text:
Use ? w:++ to append text to the end:

<name> ? w:++|<* ValuetoAppend>;

message # PT:Hello;
message ? w:++|<* text>;
message ? w:++|!;          § Appends ! to the end
message ? push:;           § Output: Hello!

docs.title: Clearing Text (Decrementing)
docs.text:
Use ? w:-- to delete all the text:

<name> ? w:--;

message # PT:Hello;
message ? w:--;            § Clears the message
message ? push:;           § Output: (empty)

docs.title: Inserting Text at a Position
docs.text:
Insert text at a specific position (position 1 is the first character):

<name> ? insertAt:<* value>|<* position>;

message # PT:Hello;
message ? insertAt:Mr. |1;    § Insert "Mr. " at the beginning
message ? push:;                 § Output: Mr. Hello

docs.title: Inserting Text at PlaceHolder
docs.text:
Inserting text at a placeholder in the original Text

<name> ? insertAt:<* placeholder>|<* text>;

message # PT:Hello $a!;
message ? insertAt:$a|World;  § Insert World at the $a
message ? push:;              § Output: Hello World!

docs.title: Getting User Input
docs.text:
Use ? INPUT: to let the user type text:

<name> ? INPUT:<*- message>;

message # PT:;
message ? INPUT:What's your name? ;
message ? push:;           § Displays what the user typed

docs.title: Check type compatibility of value ( ? check)
docs.text:
Use ? check:; to check if the current value is compatible with a variable type

value ? check:<* type>|<* targetVarName>;

value # PT:Hello;
isInt # BOOL:~0;
value ? check:INT|isInt;    § isInt is still ~0
  • type: the to check for. allowed: INT, FLOAT, BOOL
  • targetVarName: the name of the boolean var the result is pasted into.

docs.title: Creating Integer Variables
docs.text:
<name> # INT:<*- initialValue>|<*- const>;

count # INT:10;            § Create an integer
score # INT:0;             § Create with initial value 0
negative # INT:-5;         § Create negative number

docs.title: Changing Integer Value
docs.text:
<name> ? w:<* newValue>;

count # INT:10;
count ? w:20;              § Change value to 20

docs.title: Incrementing (Adding)
docs.text:
<name> ? w:++|<*- incrementValue>;

count # INT:5;
count ? w:++;              § Add 1 to count
count ? w:++|4;            § Add 4 to count
  • incrementValue defaults to 1.

docs.title: Decrementing (Subtracting)
docs.text:
<name> ? w:++|<*- decrementValue>;

count # INT:10;
count ? w:--;              § decrement count by 1
count ? w:--|4;            § decrement count by 4
  • decrementValue defaults to 1.

docs.title: Getting User Input
docs.text:
<name> ? INPUT:<*- message>;

age # INT:0;
age ? INPUT:Enter your age: ;
  • message: Message printet in the line of the input.
  • Note: If the Input doesnt match the type it throws a ZError!

docs.title: Creating Float Variables
docs.text:
pi # FLOAT:3.14;
temperature # FLOAT:-5.5;

docs.title: Displaying Floats
docs.text: pi # FLOAT:3.14159;

docs.title: Changing Float Value
docs.text:
temperature # FLOAT:20.0;
temperature ? w:25.5;      § Change temperature

docs.title: Incrementing Floats
docs.text:
temperature # FLOAT:20.0;
temperature ? w:++|<* amount>;
temperature ? w:++|0.5;    § Add 0.5 to temperature

docs.title: Decrementing Floats
docs.text:
temperature # FLOAT:20.0;
temperature ? w:--|<* amount>;
temperature ? w:--|0.5;    § Subtract 0.5 from temperature

docs.title: Getting User Input
docs.text:
weight # FLOAT:0.0;
weight ? INPUT:"Enter your weight in kg: ";

docs.title: Understanding Booleans
docs.text:
Booleans represent true/false or yes/no:

  • ~1 means true
  • ~0 means false

docs.title: Creating Boolean Variables
docs.text:
<name> # Type:<*- initialState>;

is_active # BOOL:~1;       § Create true
is_finished # BOOL:~0;     § Create false

docs.title: Toggling a Boolean
docs.text:
Use ? w:++ or ? w:-- to toggle between true and false:

<name> ? w:<++/-->;

is_active # BOOL:~1;
is_active ? w:++;          § Toggle: ~1 becomes ~0

is_active ? w:++;          § Toggle: ~0 becomes ~1

docs.title: Converting Boolean to Integer/Float
docs.text:
You can convert a boolean to INT/FLOAT:

<name> ? CT:<newType>;

is_active # BOOL:~1;
is_active ? CT:INT;        § Convert to integer
  • newType: The newType has to either be INT or FLOAT

docs.title: Conditional Object
docs.text: A Conditional Object evaluates a condition and stores the result as a boolean value. Conditional Objects are used to drive decision-making in IF statements and loops.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT, BOOL
  • convertValue -> Result of the condition

docs.title: Boolean Values
docs.text: Conditional Objects store results as boolean values: - ~1 = true - ~0 = false

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a new Conditional Object with an initial condition.

conditionalObject # CO:<*- ConditionScript>;
conditionalObject # CO:('a' > 'b');

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Replaces the condition script with a new one and re-evaluates it.

conditionalObject ? w:<*ConditionScript>;
conditionalObject ? w:('x' < 'y');

docs.title: Condition Script Format
docs.text:
Condition scripts compare two values or variables using operators. Enclose the entire condition in parentheses:

('variable1' > 'variable2')
('num1' == 'num2')
('count' < 10)

Supported operators: - > (greater than) - < (less than) - == (equal to) - != (not equal to)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Conditional Objects are typically used as arguments to IF statements and LOOP objects.
  • The result is always stored as either ~1 (true) or ~0 (false).
  • Variables used in conditions must be enclosed in single quotes.

docs.title: IF Statement
docs.text: An IF statement executes a block of code only if a Conditional Object evaluates to true. It optionally supports an ELSE block to execute code when the condition is false.

docs.title: Syntax Overview
docs.text:
if # IF:<*ConditionalObjectName>;
if ? START:;
  § Code to execute if true
if ? ELSE:;
  § Code to execute if false
if ? END:;

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> None
  • convertValue -> None

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates an IF statement with a Conditional Object reference.

if # IF:<*conditionalObjectName>;
if # IF:conditionName;

docs.title: START
docs.text:
Marks the beginning of the IF block.

if ? START:;

docs.title: ELSE
docs.text:
Marks the beginning of the ELSE block (optional). If the condition is false, these commands execute instead.

if ? ELSE:;

docs.title: END
docs.text:
Marks the end of the IF statement block.

if ? END:;

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Changes the Conditional Object that the IF statement checks.

if ? w:<*ConditionalObjectName>;
if ? w:newConditionName;

docs.title: Loop
docs.text: A Loop object repeatedly executes a block of code while a Conditional Object evaluates to true.

docs.title: Syntax Overview
docs.text:
loop # LOOP:<*ConditionalObjectName>;
loop ? START:<*CommandCount>;
  § Code to execute repeatedly
loop ? END:;

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT
  • convertValue -> Count of times Looped

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a LOOP object with a Conditional Object that controls execution.

loop # LOOP:<*ConditionalObjectName>;
loop # LOOP:conditionName;

docs.title: START
docs.text:
Marks the beginning of the loop block.

loop ? START:;

docs.title: END
docs.text:
Marks the end of the loop block.

loop ? END:;

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Changes the Conditional Object that controls whether the loop continues.

loop ? w:<*ConditionalObjectName>;
loop ? w:newConditionName;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The loop continues as long as the Conditional Object evaluates to true (~1).
  • The loop's value tracks the number of iterations completed.
  • The variable value field shows the iteration count after execution.

docs.title: List
docs.text: A List is an ordered collection of values of a single type. Its value represents the current pointer position. Lists support random access to elements and dynamic position changes.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT
  • convertValue -> Value at current Pointer position

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a new List with a specified value type.

list # LIST:<*ValueType>|<*-InitialValue1>|<*-InitialValue2>|...;
myList # LIST:INT|5|1;
  • ValueType — Type of values stored: INT, PT, or FLOAT
  • InitialValue — (Optional) initial value to store

docs.title: Set Pointer (SET)
docs.text:
Moves the pointer to a specified position. Valid positions are any integer positive and negative except 0.

list ? SET:<* position>; 
myList ? SET:2;

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Writes a value at the current pointer position.

list ? w:<*- value>; 
myList ? w:10;

docs.title: Length
docs.text:
To get the length of either the pos or neg values of a list use ? LGTH.

list ? LGTH:<* CollectionType>|<* targetVar>

length # INT:;
list ? LGTH:POS|length;
  • CollectionType: Either POS or NEG to select one of the two collections
  • targetVar: name of the Variable the length value is pasted into

docs.title: Copy
docs.text:
Copies the pos / neg values of a list to a target list with a compatible ValueType

list ? copy:<* targetVar>;

list1 # LIST:INT|1|2|3;
list2 # LIST:PT;

list1 ? copy:list2;
  • targetVar: The name of the Variable the content is pasted into.

docs.title: Reading from a List
docs.text:
To read a value from a list and assign it to another variable, use single quotes with the list name:

result ? w:'myList';              § Read value at current pointer
result ? w:'myList<2>';           § Read value at position 2
result ? w:'myList<'index'>';     § Read value at position stored in variable index
result ? w:'myList<'myList<2>'>;  § Nested list access (can nest infinitely)
  • The infinite nesting can be used everywhere a value can be replaced with a variable (*)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Pointer positions are 1-indexed and can be any integer except 0 (positive or negative).
  • Attempting to read from an unset position may result in undefined behavior.
  • List values can be read using nested variable syntax with single quotes.

docs.title: Math Object
docs.text: A Math Object evaluates a mathematical equation and stores the result. Math Objects simplify calculations and can be reused in functions or other operations. The value is calculated whenever the equation script is set or changed.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT
  • convertValue -> Result of calculation

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a new Math Object with an optional initial equation.

mathObject # MO:<*-EquationScript>;
calculation # MO:('x' + 'y' * 2);
  • EquationScript — (Optional) Mathematical expression to evaluate

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Updates the equation script and recalculates the result.

mo ? w:<*EquationScript>;
calculation ? w:('a' - 'b' / 2);

docs.title: Equation Script Format
docs.text:
Equation scripts perform arithmetic on numbers and variables. Enclose the entire equation in parentheses:

('value1' + 'value2')
('num1' - 'num2')
('x' * 'y')
('total' / 'count')

Supported operators: - + (addition) - - (subtraction) - * (multiplication) - / (division)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Math Objects calculate results only when the equation is defined or updated.
  • Variables in equations must be enclosed in single quotes.
  • The result type depends on the operand types and the operation performed.
  • Math Objects are typically used as input to Function objects.

docs.title: Function
docs.text: A Function wraps a Math Object to enable reusable calculations. Unlike Math Objects that calculate immediately, Functions only compute their result when explicitly called.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT
  • convertValue -> Result of latest calculation

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a new Function with a Math Object.

function # FUNC:<*ResultType>|<*-DisableVariableChange>|<*-MathObjectName>;
calculate # FUNC:RES|0|myMathObject;
  • ResultType — The type of the function result currently only RES
  • DisableVariableChange — (Optional) If set, the function uses variable values from declaration time only
  • MathObjectName — (Optional) Name of the Math Object to wrap

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Changes which Math Object the Function uses.

func ? w:<*MathObjectName>;
calculate ? w:newMathObject;

docs.title: Call
docs.text:
Executes the Function and computes its result based on the current Math Object.

calculate ? call:;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Functions only calculate results when call: is executed.
  • By default, Functions always use current variable values. Set the second parameter to disable this and use values from declaration time.
  • Functions are useful for organizing complex calculations and reusing computation logic.

docs.title: Random Number Generator
docs.text: An RNG (Random Number Generator) object generates a random number within a specified range. RNG objects are useful for simulations, random decisions, and game logic.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT, BOOL
  • convertValue -> Random Number generated

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a new RNG object with a specified range and output type.

rng # RNG:<*RangeMin>|<*RangeMax>|<*NumberType>;
dice # RNG:1|6|INT;
  • RangeMin — Minimum value (inclusive)
  • RangeMax — Maximum value (inclusive)
  • NumberType — Type of random number: INT or FLOAT

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Changes the range and optionally the number type.

rng ? w:<*RangeMin>|<*RangeMax>|<*- NumberType>;
dice ? w:1|20|INT;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The NumberType parameter specifies whether the random number is an INT (integer) or FLOAT (floating point).
  • The range is inclusive of both minimum and maximum values.
  • Each access to the RNG generates a new random number within the specified range.

docs.title: File Management
docs.text:
A File object manages file operations including opening, reading, writing, renaming, and deleting files. File objects allow Zephyr programs to store and manipulate external data. The variable's value is the absolute path to the file.

  • g means global - ex. change file name

  • c means content - modifies content

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> None
  • convertValue -> None

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates or opens a file at the specified path.

file # FILE:<*Path>; 
file # FILE:"data.txt";
  • Path — File path either relative or absolute (defaults to 'unnamed_file.txt' if not provided)

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Changes the file path to point to a different file.

file ? w:<*Path>;
file ? w:"newfile.txt";

docs.title: Set Content (cSET)
docs.text:
Writes content to the file, replacing any existing content.

file ? cSET:<*Content>;
file ? cSET:"Hello, World!";

docs.title: Clear Content (cFLUSH)
docs.text:
Removes all content from the file.

file ? cFLUSH:;

docs.title: Read Content (cREAD)
docs.text:
Reads the content of a file and loads it onto a PT or LIST

file ? cREAD:<* targetVarname>|<*- cleanLines>;

content # LIST:;
file ? cREAD:content|~1;
  • targetVarName: name of the variable the content is pasted into. Allowed Types are: PT, LIST. At PT the lines are combined to a single string. At LIST the lines are split and put on the Positive List Collection
  • cleanLines: Boolean switch if the lines are cleaned (remove Whitespaces, linebreaks)

docs.title: Rename (gRENAME)
docs.text:
Renames the file to a new name.

file ? gRENAME:<*NewName>;
file ? gRENAME:"renamed.txt";

docs.title: Delete (gDEL)
docs.text:
Deletes the file permanently.

file ? gDEL:;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The file's value field stores the absolute path to the current file.
  • Writing with cSET overwrites all existing file content.
  • Use cFLUSH to clear content without deleting the file itself.
  • File names should include the file extension (e.g., .txt, .log).
  • All path operations are system-dependent.

docs.title: Built-In
docs.text: Built-in commands provide core functionality for program flow control, timing, persistence, and library imports. The built-in variable is always named __ and its state cannot be changed.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> None
  • convertValue -> None

docs.title: Wait
docs.text:
Pauses program execution for a specified number of seconds.

__ ? wait:<*SecondsToWait>;
__ ? wait:2;

docs.title: Jump (Relative)
docs.text:
Moves execution to a relative position in the code (forward or backward).

__ ? jump:<*RelativePositionInCode>;
__ ? jump:5;

docs.title: Jump To (Absolute)
docs.text:
Moves execution to an absolute line number in the code.

__ ? jumpTo:<*AbsolutePositionInCode>;
__ ? jumpTo:10;

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
Saves variables to an export file. Defaults to .zpkg with the same name as the source file.

__ ? export:<*ExportPath>;  
__ ? export:"variables.zpkg";

docs.title: Load
docs.text:
Imports variables from a previously exported file.

__ ? load:<*ImportPath>;
__ ? load:"variables.zpkg";

docs.title: Library (LIB)
docs.text:
Imports and loads an external library file.

__ ? LIB:<*LibFilePath>;
__ ? LIB:"./lib/GPIO.py";

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The __ variable is reserved and built-in—it cannot be renamed or redeclared.
  • Jump commands count executable lines. Blank lines and comments do not count.
  • Export/Load operations use the .zpkg format.
  • Libraries must be located at the specified file path relative to the current working directory.
  • Library files typically have .py extensions as they are Python-based.

docs.title: Animation Object
docs.text: An Animation Object renders animated frames inside the terminal. It supports frame management, timing control, and playback operations to create sequential animations.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• convertibleInto -> PT, INT, FLOAT
  • convertValue -> Current animation position

docs.title: Define
docs.text:
Creates a new Animation Object with optional initial settings.

ao # AO:<*- initialFrame>|<*- delayInSeconds>|<*- clearScreen>;
animation # AO:"Frame1"|2|~1;
  • InitialFrame — (Optional) Initial frame as PT or type that can convert into PT
  • DelayInSeconds — (Optional) Delay between frames in seconds
  • ClearScreen — (Optional) Whether to clear screen after each frame: ~0 (false) or ~1 (true)

docs.title: Write (w)
docs.text:
Adds a new frame to the animation. Accepts any type that can convert to PT.

ao ? w:<*FrameToAdd>;
animation ? w:"Frame2";

docs.title: WriteList (wLIST)
docs.text:
adds a new Frame to the animation. accepts the name of a LIST

ao ? wLIST:<* ListVarName>;
animation ? w:myList;

docs.title: Set Delay (setDelay)
docs.text:
Updates the delay between animation frames. Accepts an integer representing seconds.

ao ? setDelay:<*seconds>;
animation ? setDelay:1;

docs.title: Clear Screen (clearScreen)
docs.text:
Enables or disables screen clearing between frames. Accepts ~1 (true) or ~0 (false).

ao ? clearScreen:<*bool>;
animation ? clearScreen:~1;

docs.title: Set Index (setIndex)
docs.text:
Jumps to a specific frame by index and displays it. Accepts an integer.

ao ? setIndex:<*index>; 
animation ? setIndex:3;

docs.title: Start
docs.text:
Begins playing the animation from the current index. After completion, restarts from index 0.

animation ? start:;

docs.title: Step
docs.text:
Advances to the next frame and displays it without starting automatic playback.

animation ? step:;

docs.title: Display
docs.text:
Shows the current frame without changing the index.

animation ? display:;

docs.title: Reset
docs.text:
Resets the index to 0 without displaying any frames.

animation ? reset:;

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The animation index starts at 0 for the first frame.
  • Delay is specified in seconds.
  • Screen clearing applies after each frame is displayed during playback.
  • The start method plays through all frames and then restarts from frame 0.
  • Frames can be strings (PT type) or list elements converted to text.
